title: Generic classes
successMessage: One Box class, three different types. The compiler tracks the type — strBox.get() returns a String, intBox.get() returns an Integer. No casting, no runtime errors.

title: Generic methods
hint: static <T> void printArray(T[] arr) { for (T item : arr) System.out.print(item + " "); System.out.println(); }
successMessage: The same printArray and reverse work on String[], Integer[], or any array. The compiler infers T from the argument — you don't need to specify <String> explicitly.

title: Bounded type parameters
successMessage: <T extends Number> restricts T to Number and subclasses — Integer, Double, Long all qualify. <T extends Comparable<T>> ensures compareTo() is available. Bounded types unlock powerful reusable algorithms.

title: Wildcards — ? for flexible methods
instruction:
Wildcards handle situations where you don't know or don't care about the exact type:

- `List<?>` — any type (read-only)
- `List<? extends Number>` — Number or subclass (producer)
- `List<? super Integer>` — Integer or parent (consumer)

The PECS rule: Producer Extends, Consumer Super.
successMessage: List<?> accepts any List. List<? extends Number> accepts Integer, Double, etc. The ? extends version gives you read access to elements as Number.

title: Build a generic stack
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build `class Stack<T>`:
- `private` storage using an array (hint: use `Object[]` internally since Java can't create generic arrays directly)
- `push(T item)` — add to top, grow if full
- `pop()` — remove and return top, throw `EmptyStackException` if empty
- `peek()` — view top without removing
- `isEmpty()` / `size()`

Test with both `Stack<String>` and `Stack<Integer>`.
hint: Use ArrayList<T> internally instead if arrays with generics feel tricky. It's the same idea.
successMessage: A type-safe generic stack. Stack<String> only holds Strings, Stack<Integer> only integers. The compiler enforces this — no ClassCastException possible. Chapter 14 done!
